sources.notes: Used to read the statement when the primary Codeforces host was protected by Cloudflare.
Used to confirm the lower-bound construction and the all-one-winner special case.

reference_candidates.notes: 洛谷题解候选来源；只有实际参考后才移动到 sources。

annotation.summary: Given party vote totals, district minimum populations, and the required winning party in each district, decide whether nonnegative district allocations can realize all strict majorities.
annotation.constraints: Sum of n over tests is 2e5, while x, y, and p_i are up to 1e9, so the feasibility conditions must be checked with linear scans and 64-bit sums.
annotation.core_idea: Each district whose designated winner has minimum population p_i needs at least floor(p_i/2)+1 votes from that winner. If both parties win at least one district, after satisfying these lower bounds any leftover votes can be absorbed by a district won by that party. If all districts have the same winner, the losing party can only be absorbed while maintaining a strict majority in every district, which adds the global difference condition winner_votes >= loser_votes + n.

solution_variants.name: main
solution_variants.summary: Check total capacity, sum per-party lower bounds floor(p_i/2)+1, then split into mixed-winner and single-winner cases.

tags.evidence: The feasibility test is reduced to a small set of necessary and sufficient inequalities on x, y, sum p_i, and the strict-majority margins.
tags.description: Reasoning with lower/upper bounds, strict inequalities, or feasibility inequalities that directly characterize existence.
tags.created_reason: Useful for constructive feasibility problems where the whole solution is deriving exact inequalities rather than running an algorithm.

tags.evidence: The proof argues by constructing a valid allocation from lower-bound votes and showing how leftovers can be placed.
The decisive branch is whether both parties have designated districts or all districts share the same designated winner.
The solution is an arithmetic feasibility proof using sums and strict-majority bounds.
